items.title: Meth, steroids and sparkly iPhone found in grass-disguised package dropped in B.C. prison yard
items.summary_auto: Police suspect the large package was flown by drone over the Mission Institution at night and dropped into the prison yard.

items.title: SPCA calls for stronger protection of animals under N.W.T. legislation
items.summary_auto: The N.W.T. SPCA says it’s gotten concerns from tourists and residents alike for years over the care and conditions of animals, and the current legislation limits how and when authorities can intervene to protect an animal.

items.title: Alberta's top court overturns conviction for man who beat his partner to death
items.summary_auto: Citing a legal error, a panel of three judges at Alberta’s highest court has ordered a new trial for Ryan Applegarth, a man convicted of second-degree murder six years ago for the fatal beating of Chantelle Firingstoney, his common-law wife.

items.title: Toronto mall security employee shot after confronting alleged jewelry store robber: police
items.summary_auto: A Fairview Mall security employee was rushed to hospital Tuesday after being shot while confronting a man who had just robbed a jewelry store, Toronto police say.

items.title: The female anglerfish's shiny lure may be as irresistible to her lovers as it is to her prey
items.summary_auto: Scientists have long known that deepsea female anglerfish use their glowing lures to draw in unwitting prey. Now, a new study posits the appendage may have an additional function — as a beacon for lonely little males, looking for love in the dark abyss.

items.title: Anthropic's latest AI model is sparking fears from cybersecurity experts and the banking sector. Here's why.
items.summary_auto: Mythos, Anthropic's most advanced AI model to date, has sparked fears about the threat to traditional software security after the AI ‌startup said the preview had uncovered "thousands" of major vulnerabilities in "every major operating system and web browser."

items.title: Calgary researchers call for national database tracking physician sexual assault, misconduct
items.summary_auto: Researchers from the University of Calgary say they've found key gaps in the way allegations and findings of sexual assault and misconduct against physicians are tracked and reported in Canada — and they argue that’s putting patients at risk.

items.title: N.L. budget will be delivered on April 29
items.summary_auto: The Newfoundland and Labrador government says it will table its budget for the upcoming fiscal year on April 29.

items.title: Ottawa has asked Israel to investigate Canadian's killing in Lebanon: Anand
items.summary_auto: Canadian officials have summoned  Israeli ambassador Iddo Moed to request an investigation into the killing of Mohammad Hassan Haidar, a Canadian citizen in southern Lebanon, Foreign Affairs Minister Anita Anand said.

items.title: 2 Palestinians, including 14-year-old, killed in Israeli settler attack in West Bank: health officials
items.summary_auto: Two Palestinians, including a 14-year-old boy, were killed in the Israeli-occupied West Bank on Tuesday after Israeli settlers and soldiers opened fire, witnesses said, while in Gaza health officials said two Palestinians were killed in Israeli strikes.

items.title: Canada's women's soccer team rises to 9th in rankings following impressive performance at FIFA Series
items.summary_auto: Canada's women's soccer team moved up one place to ninth in the FIFA rankings released Tuesday after a two-win showing at the recent FIFA Series in Brazil.

items.title: Alberta premier denies claims of UCP interference on electoral boundaries
items.summary_auto: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is rejecting Opposition accusations that her United Conservatives are pulling the strings to rejig election ridings in their favour.

items.title: Drake teases album release, prompts crowd chaos with massive ice blocks in downtown Toronto
items.summary_auto: Somewhere inside a massive formation of ice blocks in a downtown Toronto parking lot lies the answer many hip-hop fans have been waiting for: the release date of Drake's forthcoming album.

items.title: B.C. Ferries alters Northern Expedition schedule amid efforts to avoid hitting humpback whales
items.summary_auto: The Northern Expedition ferry, serving Prince Rupert and Haida Gwaii, is travelling at lower speeds in an effort to reduce the risk of hitting humpback whales and other marine mammals in the region.

items.title: Nunavut man wins volleyball title after surviving 3 days in blizzard on snowmobile trip to tournament
items.summary_auto: Peter Qayutinuak Jr. was part of a group of five Taloyoak, Nunavut, residents who were headed for Gjoa Haven on snowmobiles on Apr. 7 for a volleyball tournament. There was a blizzard and they tried to beat it, but Qayutinuak ended up getting separated from the group, and he wasn't found for another three days.

items.title: Ex-official blames pressure from U.K. prime minister's office to pick Epstein-linked U.S. ambassador
items.summary_auto: A former top Foreign Ministry official said on Tuesday he had faced "constant pressure" from U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer's office to speed up the process to install his pick as U.S. ambassador, deepening a row that threatens the British leader.

items.title: Manitoba municipality has no authority to ban 'disruptive' resident from council meetings, judge rules
items.summary_auto: A judge has told a municipality in eastern Manitoba it cannot ban a resident it considers disruptive from attending council meetings.

items.title: Century-old Winnipeg clothing manufacturer gets a new chapter from Brokenhead Ojibway Nation purchasing it
items.summary_auto: Brokenhead Ojibway Nation's purchase of clothing manufacturer Freed & Freed International will create economic and procurement opportunities within the industry, as well as an opportunity to provide jobs and skills, Chief Gordon BlueSky says.

items.title: Can liberal democracy be saved?
items.summary_auto: Is liberal democracy dying? If so, can it be revived? A conversation with Nobel laureate Daron Acemoglu.

items.title: First Nation buys Winnipeg clothing manufacturer with plans to expand federal, provincial contracts
items.summary_auto: Brokenhead Ojibway Nation's purchase of clothing manufacturer Freed & Freed International will create economic and procurement opportunities within the industry, as well as an opportunity to provide jobs and skills, Chief Gordon BlueSky says.

items.title: Knives, box cutters, even a hacksaw. What weapon detectors uncovered at the Halifax Infirmary
items.summary_auto: Devices that look for weapons or items that could be used as a weapon were installed at the Halifax Infirmary following a stabbing last year. Some of the items now in the hospital's possession include knives, box cutters, a hacksaw and what one official calls "improvised weapons."

items.title: Cost of living, financial pressures behind a year of contract turmoil at N.S. universities
items.summary_auto: Cost-of-living pressures, changing demographics and financial constraints are some of the factors contributing to the past year of contract discord at several Nova Scotia universities.

items.title: Drone seeding gives P.E.I. farmers a head start on season
items.summary_auto: A growing number of P.E.I. farmers are using agricultural drones to give them a head start on the season. Spring time is muddy on the Island, making it almost impossible for heavy trucks and tractors to get into fields. But the drone is adding a couple of extra weeks to get work done before planting. Nancy Russell and drone operator Aaron Adetuyi have the story.

items.title: These Toronto residents say there's already too much plane exhaust. Doug Ford wants to bring in bigger jets
Who was Sir Isaac Brock, and why does Carney have his statuette in the Prime Minister's Office?
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney stunned some Canadian historians over the weekend when he released a pointed video message about the U.S. featuring a War of 1812 general who led the charge against the American invaders of that era.

items.title: Family of Indigenous woman who died in Ontario hospital after 2 ER visits seeks homicide ruling at inquest
items.summary_auto: The family of an Indigenous woman is urging an inquest jury examining her death in an Ontario hospital to rule it was a homicide due to biases, and errors made in her assessment and treatment. Heather Winterstein, 24, went to the St. Catharines hospital on Dec. 9, 2021, but a doctor ruled out infection, opted against doing bloodwork and attributed her symptoms to "social issues," the inquest heard. She returned the next day and died of sepsis after hours in the waiting room.

items.title: The future of electricity is wind and solar, new report says. Canada is lagging behind
items.summary_auto: Renewable energy met all new demand for electricity in 2025, according to a new review of global power generation, preventing any increase in the burning of fossil fuels to produce electricity and highlighting the promise of clean sources like wind and solar.

items.title: Marineland requesting $10M-$20M federal loan to export belugas to U.S.
items.summary_auto: CBC News has learned from a senior government source that Marineland — the permanently closed theme park and zoo in Niagara Falls, Ont. — is requesting $10 million to $20 million in a federal loan to move up to 30 belugas and four dolphins to aquariums across the U.S.

items.title: Mexico releases new details in Teotihuacán shooting that killed Canadian woman
Carney to name new Canada-U.S. trade advisory council, sources say
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney will name a new advisory council on Canada and U.S. trade on Tuesday, according to two sources familiar with the matter.
